Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitive verbal phrase with object noun and locative prepositional phrase; metaphorical fixed expression.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To calm down, be patient, and hold oneself back before acting.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Kukala ni kixikanu; kulemba kukwata nzambi.
Usage Contexts: family advice, daily speech, conflict resolution, workplace patience
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Built on the image of the heart as the seat of emotion; elders in Luanda and inland communities often advise restraint before public disagreement.
First Example Sentence: Mona, tula muxima mu maku, kadi makamba ma ku rijila.
Second Example Sentence: Mu musoso wa ndenge, mwene watula muxima mu maku nê waza ku zwela.
Third Example Sentence: Kioso kiwila makuetu, tula muxima mu maku o zwela ni kixikanu.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitive transitive verb phrase with possessed noun phrase; semi-fixed metaphor of path and conduct.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To behave properly, choose the right course, or make a wise decision.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Kusola kima kiambote; kubhanga mu kixikanu.
Usage Contexts: school, parenting, moral advice, community life
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The path metaphor is widespread in Kimbundu moral teaching, linking travel, social responsibility, and personal reputation.
First Example Sentence: Ngana mesene walonga ana kutu kukwata njila ya mbote.
Second Example Sentence: Mu kilunga, muntu u kwata njila ya mbote u zola kuzwela ni ukaxi.
Third Example Sentence: Se wenda ku Luanda, kwata njila ya mbote mu ima yoso.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitive verb phrase with concrete object and genitive complement; figurative food-metaphor construction.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To gain wisdom through hard experience or suffering.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Kulonguka ku ndenge ya makasi; kukula mu ndunge.
Usage Contexts: life lessons, elder speech, literature, reflection after hardship
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Uses the experience of eating bitter things as a metaphor for learning; resonates with oral traditions where hardship produces discernment.
First Example Sentence: Kimbundu kia xitu kiamutene kudia malavu ma ndunge.
Second Example Sentence: Emi ngi dia malavu ma ndunge, nê kiki ngi zola kuzwela kiambote.
Third Example Sentence: Mukulu wami wazwela: mundu u dia malavu ma ndunge ka lunga kuxinga.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitive stative verb with comitative phrase and numeral; ironic fixed phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To pretend affection while hiding another intention; to act two-faced.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Kukala ni makanisi mawiri; kuzola mu mesu kaka.
Usage Contexts: sarcasm, politics, friendship disputes, family tensions
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Draws on the symbolism of double-sided behavior; often used when public friendliness contrasts with private betrayal.
First Example Sentence: Kadi u mu kwijila; mwene u zola ni mazanga mawiri.
Second Example Sentence: Mu makambu ma soba, mundu u zola ni mazanga mawiri ka tala kixima kia mukwenu.
Third Example Sentence: Ame ngi mona o mukongo wendê: u seka, nê u zola ni mazanga mawiri.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitive transitive verb phrase with object and locative body-part phrase; directness idiom.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To speak frankly and tell someone the truth without hiding it.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Kuzwela kiatunda; kuleka kufika o kieleka.
Usage Contexts: meetings, family correction, leadership, honest advice
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Face-to-face truth-telling is valued in mediation, especially when elders or leaders restore social clarity.
First Example Sentence: Soba watema kima mu mesu ku jinjila ja kilunga.
Second Example Sentence: Ngana João watema kima mu mesu, kadi o maka ma lenda kubula ukamba.
Third Example Sentence: Se u zola kieleka, tema kima mu mesu nê u bika kuxikila.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitive verb phrase with body-part object and cause/source complement; metaphorical cleansing phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To free oneself from blame, suspicion, or responsibility.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Kumonisa kieleka; kuleka kulanduka mu maka.
Usage Contexts: legal speech, workplace disputes, community mediation, politics
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Connected to symbolic ideas of clean hands and public accountability in communal judgment.
First Example Sentence: Mu kima kia tribunal, mwene wasukula maku ku mayela moso.
Second Example Sentence: Kikulu kia empresa kizola kusukula maku ku mayela ma kifuxi.
Third Example Sentence: Kana u kala ni kieleka, sukula maku ku mayela mu mesu ma makamba.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitive intransitive verb with instrumental/comitative noun phrase; simile-like fixed metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To speak loudly, forcefully, or with intimidating authority.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Kuzwela ni nguzu; kuzwela mu ndaka inene.
Usage Contexts: public speaking, anger, leadership, storytelling, humor
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Thunder imagery evokes power and awe, familiar in oral description of chiefs, elders, storms, and heated public speech.
First Example Sentence: Tata wazwela ni ndaka ya nzaji, ana oso bavutuka.
Second Example Sentence: Mu zandu, mukaji wazwela ni ndaka ya nzaji sambu a mu iva.
Third Example Sentence: Kana u zola kulonga, kuzwela ni ndaka ya nzaji ku lenda kusosa muxima.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Copular/stative construction with possessed body-part noun phrase and animal genitive; descriptive idiom.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To be very watchful, alert, and hard to deceive.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Kukala ni kutala kwambote; kubika kufundisa.
Usage Contexts: market life, security, parenting, travel, praise
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The antelope/deer image reflects alertness in local ecological memory and hunting narratives.
First Example Sentence: Mu zandu ya Kinaxixi, mama u kala ni mesu ma ngulungu.
Second Example Sentence: Kambuta ka mu fundisa, kadi mwene u kala ni mesu ma ngulungu.
Third Example Sentence: Mutu wa kilunga u kala ni mesu ma ngulungu kana kuiza kima kia mbi.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitive verb phrase with abstract object and locative heart complement; emotional containment idiom.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To keep resentment, grief, or a secret inside without showing it.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Kufika maka mu ndani; kubika kuzwela o mpasi.
Usage Contexts: family conflict, mourning, secrets, literature, intimate conversation
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects social expectations around discretion, emotional dignity, and the heart as a container of hidden feeling.
First Example Sentence: Mu lumbu ya kufwa kwa tata, mama wabika kixima mu muxima.
Second Example Sentence: Kuzola kubika kixima mu muxima kulenda kubebesa muenhu.
Third Example Sentence: Mwene ka zwela ko, kadi o kixima kiala mu muxima wendê.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Reflexive infinitive verb phrase with locative complement and possessive genitive; cautionary metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To interfere in another person’s affairs and get oneself into trouble.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Kuingila mu maka ma mukwenu; kudibwisa mu kufuxika.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Water imagery evokes boundaries, ownership, and risk; the idiom warns against crossing social limits without invitation.
First Example Sentence: Kana u dikuata mu makambu ma jixi, u dibeeta mu madi ma mundu.
Second Example Sentence: Mukwa-maka wadibeta mu madi ma mundu, nê kiki wamusengela.
Third Example Sentence: Ene baseka, kadi ngi mu lombolola: kubika kudibeta mu madi ma mundu.
